metadata.title: “We Are Bulletproof”: The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #<scp>StopAsianHate</scp>

abstract:
Fears of COVID-19, alongside existing anti-Asian sentiment, resulted in 11,467 documented hate incidents against Asians/Asian Americans in the United States between March 2020 and March 2022, per the Stop AAPI Hate Reporting Center. In response, #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate were used on Twitter to increase public awareness. Considering anti-Asian racism transcends geographic boundaries, we interrogate: how does engagement with these hashtags reflect the transculturality of responses to anti-Asian racism in a digital environment? We analyzed 920,271 tweets between March 19, 2020, and May 31, 2022, containing either hashtag, defining a "spike" in engagement as any day with >5,000 tweets, and found that of 25 spikes, 11 immediately followed the 2021 Atlanta-area shootings-however, another 12 spikes were related to South Korean megagroup BTS. We further compare the results of two topic modeling methods, LDA and NMF, to examine how the use of and engagement with these hashtags shift over time primarily due to BTS fans ("ARMY"). This work joins emerging scholarship on race, fandom, and activism through cultural production in order to advance our collective understanding of modern digital activism and racial politics. Overall, we interrogate fandom's contributions to combating anti-Asian sentiment, build on existing literature exploring the potential for fandoms to advocate for social justice, and argue that the transculturality of BTS, ARMY, and their affective ties represent an opportunity for accessible global discourse about anti-Asian racism.

Nearly 2 weeks following the murders of eight people-six women of Asian descent, a white woman, and a white man-at Atlanta-area massage parlors, South Korean megagroup BTS posted what became the most retweeted tweet of 2021 (Twitter Data 2021). In a post simply stating "#StopAsianHate #StopAAPIHate," the group accompanied their tweet with two screenshots of a statement in Korean and English condemning the surmounting violence against Asians while simultaneously reflecting on their own experiences with anti-Asian racism (@bts_twt, 2021, March 29).

Just over 1 year prior to their statement, the World Health Organization declared COVID-19 a global pandemic on March 11, 2020. Since then, ramifications from the ongoing health crisis have touched almost every aspect of everyday life, exposing weaknesses within societal institutions and so-called safety nets with minority and immigrant populations largely shouldering the burden of increased morbidity and mortality, resulting in increased disparities in physical and mental health (

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: "What is happening right now cannot be dissociated from our identity as Asians. . . We stand against racial discrimination. We condemn violence. You, I and we all have the right to be respected. We will stand together."

-@bts_twt (2021, March 29) widespread social upheaval, the world witnessed an intensification of anti-Asian rhetoric, harassment, and violence with news stories chronicling these increases in Australia, Brazil, Canada, New Zealand, the UK, and other parts of the world (Haynes 2021;Horse et al. 2022;Human Rights Watch 2020;Tessler, Choi, and Kao 2020).

As anti-Asian sentiment is increasingly digitally transmitted via popular media outlets and on social media platforms, new digital strategies are required to intervene against them. Largely in response to the March 2021 Atlanta-area shootings, people on social media shared their reflections on the rise in anti-Asian racism sparked by COVID-19 using the hashtags #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate. 1 Since people rely on social media networks to amplify messaging and facilitate discourse, it is critical for scholars to examine how modern networked publics (Boyd 2010) inform how people engage with topics of racism, even as the nature of online cultural landscapes prompts frequent content turnover. Considering anti-Asian racism exists on a global scale in digitally mediated environments, we ask: how does the use of #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate, hashtags that were primarily popularized through Western media, reflect the transculturality of responses to anti-Asian racism in a digital environment?

In order to explore how the affordances of networked publics impact discourse about anti-Asian racism, we use a Python tool, Twint, to scrape publicly available tweets between January 2020 and May 2022 that use at least one of the #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPI-Hate hashtags to obtain a corpus of data for analysis. Although both hashtags were created out of a desire to combat anti-Asian rhetoric broadly, our descriptive analysis suggests the continued usage of these hashtags following March 2021 is largely driven by ARMY, the fandom of K-pop megagroup BTS. We corroborate this using two different topic modeling methods in order to disaggregate what subjects were being discussed. Our results prompt an investigation of digital activism vis-a-vis fandom, particularly across transcultural boundaries and considering the universality of anti-Asian racism and its corresponding countermovements. Accordingly, this study interrogates fandom's contributions to combating anti-Asian sentiment, builds on existing literature exploring the potential for fandoms to advocate for social justice (Brough and Shresthova 2012;Cho 2022;Gray, Sandvoss, and Harrington 2020;Lee et al. 2022;Lopez 2016;McLaren and Jin 2020;Morimoto and Chin 2017), and argues that the transculturality of BTS, ARMY, and their affective ties represent an opportunity for accessible global discourse about anti-Asian racism.

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: Racializing Asians in the West: The Case of BTS

The transnational spread of Korean culture broadly, known as the Hallyu wave, has been documented since the late 1990s. Existing research on the K-pop industry demonstrates the transnational and transcultural impact of K-pop artists, from uncovering the historical linkages between early South Korean artists and Black American military members during the Korean War (Kim 2021a) to examining the popularity of K-pop in Spanish-speaking countries (Choi and Park 2014;Han 2017;Vargas Meza and Park 2015). In recent years, the phenomenon of "Hallyu 2.0" is characterized by the popularity of Korean pop music, or Kpop, in Western markets alongside the mobilization of K-pop fandom and the spread of Korean culture online, propelled forward by social media platforms (Jin 2018;Jin and Yoon 2016). Hallyu 2.0, and South Korea's emergence in the late 1990s and into the 2000s as a new global competitor more broadly highlights a predicament for the US empire. While South Korea currently operates as a powerful ally to the United States, their continuing global popularity threatens Western dominance (Lee 2016), and the rising popularity of Kpop artists on a global scale has not shielded these artists from racist rhetoric. Instead, orientalizing racial logic is embedded in the ways that K-pop artists and their rise to international acclaim are made legible to an international audience. Broadly, Asians and Asian Americans in the US oscillate from model minority to perpetual foreigner (Okihiro 2014;Wu 2014), with both categorizations requiring the regulation and surveillance of Asian bodies-how Kpop artists are perceived is no exception.

In September 2020, Bangtan Sonyeondan, or BTS, rose to the top of the Billboard Hot 100, the US' music industry standard record chart for songs based on sales, online streaming, and radio play, with "Dynamite." This was a first for a South Korean act, which BTS followed with five more #1 songs in the next 13 months. Despite BTS' widespread influence in the West, including five Grammy nominations (no other K-pop group has any), the group has frequently been the target of both casual and intentional acts of discrimination that target their masculinity, their race, or both (Lee 2022). Although Western media highlighted the group as they became popular, they were represented "as an exotic, foreign act, rather than. . .[a] reflect[ion of] the influence of American pop aesthetics and youth cultural ethos across the Asian Pacific" (Cho 2022:271). BTS' racial positioning in the West places suspicion on their ability to become one of the biggest musical acts in the world-the ways that the group is widely celebrated while concurrently victimized through processes of racialization emulates the ways that Asians/Asian Americans broadly are "simultaneously rejected and embraced" in a new media environment (Lopez 2016:16). For example, after BTS' fourth Billboard Hot 100 #1, the group was accused by critics of manipulating their chart performance, despite no official reports of suspicious chart activity (Price 2021). Oftentimes, those who take issue with BTS are threatened by their growing global and transcultural popularity, as it runs in direct contradiction to the West's position as the center of cultural production. Many of these critics ascribe reasonings for BTS' popularity that reify Orientalist fantasies (Kim 2021b;Suh 2021), or are willing to dismiss them based on their Koreanness without considering their talent or music (Cruz 2019). Particularly in the midst of the COVID-19 pandemic, when their popularity increased significantly, BTS functions as a racialized site, or a proxy for Asian bodies en masse-a potential racial threat that "renders them vulnerable to racial discrimination, aggression, and even violence" (Li and Nicholson 2021:5).

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: BTS' rise to global prominence can be credited in large part to the efforts of their fanbase, ARMY (Adorable Representative M.C. for Youth), and their commitment to digital strategies on social network sites (SNS). SNS (Boyd and Ellison 2007) can be understood using a framework of "networked publics," or publics defined by both a digital space constructed through networked technologies and an "imagined collective that emerges as a result of the intersection of people, technology, and practice" (Boyd 2010:39). As a global and transcultural fanbase present in over 100 countries (Grover et al. 2022), ARMY operates as a networked public, utilizing digital spaces to find camaraderie in not only the shared affection for their idols but also a communal pursuit of similar values including social justice, in many cases (Jin 2021). These values were foundational to BTS themselves, given the first TRANSCULTURAL FANDOM IN #STOPASIANHATE 393 three members, BTS' rappers, were reverent students of hip-hop-which "as an artistic and discursive practice is deeply political, often subaltern and counterhegemonic" (Zou 2019:182). This history arguably informed the social consciousness they regularly infuse into songs, and over the years, BTS has constructed a reputation as one of K-pop's most socially conscious groups, with some of their earliest lyrics speaking to issues of youth such as critiques of the education system and consumerism (Jin 2021). The resulting discourse that unfolds on SNS through networked publics like those of ARMY is worth analyzing as an archive of real-time engagement with culture and society (Jackson, Bailey, and Welles 2020;Kuo 2018).

As exemplified through digital environments, K-pop fandom differs from Western music fandoms in unique ways. While East Asian pop idols generally cultivate forms of fanartist codependency described by some as modeling parasociality (Cho 2022), such fan-toartist engagement promotes a response largely unique to K-pop fans. Fans actively follow the activities of their artist online, seeking out ways to ensure their success alongside promoting other strategies of fan activism such as bus advertising and online petitions (Jung 2012). Such dedication manifests in digital promotional techniques and campaignsfor example, one common strategy across fandoms is to post tweets en masse using agreedupon specific hashtags in order to create trending topics that are noticeable to the "general public," becoming a topic of interest for parties who may not have been originally invested (Abidin and Lee 2023). K-pop fan practices are institutionalized as forms of effective science communication, where knowing how to identify potential misinformation, attract wider audiences, and constantly adapt to changing digital practices (such as evolving rules for streaming sites or charting) demonstrates digital media literacy and knowledge gained through time and effort (Abidin 2019;Lee et al. 2022).

The values that inform the fan-artist relationship between ARMY and BTS lead to a particularly unique form of fan-celebrity intimacy that offers the potential for increased engagement beyond a digital space (Cho 2022). Recent studies looking at BTS' role as drivers of social change (Chang, Pham, and Ferrara 2021;Cho 2022;Jin 2021;Lee and Kao 2021;Rooks 2022;Youn 2023) and ARMY's affective ties (McLaren and Jin 2020) emphasize the importance of fandom in propagating and sustaining online movement activities. With ARMY encompassing people across all genders, races, and ages in addition to geographic diversity, their investments and interests in movements are broad but generally progressive. Politically, ARMY has impacted recent elections in Chile, the Philippines, and the US by "organizing acts of civil resistance" and "effectively us[ing] their digital skills" (Youn 2023). Notably, in June 2020, ARMY successfully raised $1 million for racial justice organization Black Lives Matter within 24 hours to match a $1 million donation from BTS and their management company (Cho 2022). Such efforts, unparalleled in scale by other Kpop fanbases, are largely possible through the sheer scale and dedication of ARMY, a fanbase that is difficult to properly quantify even as the group boasts 76.9 million subscribers on YouTube and 48.5 million followers on Twitter.

Outside of K-pop, the study of fandoms as transcultural networked publics capable of initiating social change is not new (Chin and Morimoto 2013;Lopez 2016;Morimoto and Chin 2017). Although fan activities focus on idols and particular cultural productions or franchises, fandom is not devoid of relevance to the real world, as "fandom is always performed against a backdrop of real-world events, constraints, and subjectivities" (Morimoto and Chin 2017:181). Relevant work in the field of fan studies examines the ways that celebrities themselves engage their fans on social media to advance philanthropic causes (Bennett 2014), how fans mobilize to support celebrities and cultural productions (Hopkins 2022; Noh 2020; Riddick 2022), how fans engage in political participation (Brough and Shresthova 2012;Jenkins and Shresthova 2012), and how both sport and non-sport fans form communities among one another based on both personal and social identity (Reysen and Branscombe 2010). Considering the growing nature of K-pop as a global phenomenon and fandom's socio-political activism across borders, our work furthers research examining fandom and K-pop artists as "inseparable and symbiotic entities" (Cho 2022:271) that interact to build a sense of "synergistic virality" (Chang, Pham, and Ferrara 2021). In particular, the forced digital environment created by COVID-19 and subsequent government-mandated lockdowns presents a unique landscape for analyzing how people engaged with #StopAsian-Hate and #StopAAPIHate, even if such engagement was motivated by fandom.

Considering the growing nature of K-pop as a global phenomenon and fandom's socio-political activism across borders, our work furthers research examining fandom and K-pop artists as "inseparable and symbiotic entities" (Cho 2022:271) that interact to build a sense of "synergistic virality" (Chang, Pham, and Ferrara 2021). In particular, the forced digital environment created by COVID-19 and subsequent government-mandated lockdowns presents a unique landscape for analyzing how people engaged with #StopAsian-Hate and #StopAAPIHate, even if such engagement was motivated by fandom.

BTS' rise to global prominence can be credited in large part to the efforts of their fanbase, ARMY (Adorable Representative M.C. for Youth), and their commitment to digital strategies on social network sites (SNS). SNS (Boyd and Ellison 2007) can be understood using a framework of "networked publics," or publics defined by both a digital space constructed through networked technologies and an "imagined collective that emerges as a result of the intersection of people, technology, and practice" (Boyd 2010:39). As a global and transcultural fanbase present in over 100 countries (Grover et al. 2022), ARMY operates as a networked public, utilizing digital spaces to find camaraderie in not only the shared affection for their idols but also a communal pursuit of similar values including social justice, in many cases (Jin 2021). These values were foundational to BTS themselves, given the first TRANSCULTURAL FANDOM IN #STOPASIANHATE 393 three members, BTS' rappers, were reverent students of hip-hop-which "as an artistic and discursive practice is deeply political, often subaltern and counterhegemonic" (Zou 2019:182). This history arguably informed the social consciousness they regularly infuse into songs, and over the years, BTS has constructed a reputation as one of K-pop's most socially conscious groups, with some of their earliest lyrics speaking to issues of youth such as critiques of the education system and consumerism (Jin 2021). As exemplified through digital environments, K-pop fandom differs from Western music fandoms in unique ways. While East Asian pop idols generally cultivate forms of fanartist codependency described by some as modeling parasociality (Cho 2022), such fan-toartist engagement promotes a response largely unique to K-pop fans. Fans actively follow the activities of their artist online, seeking out ways to ensure their success alongside promoting other strategies of fan activism such as bus advertising and online petitions (Jung 2012). sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
sections.header_norm: anti asian racism online amidst covid 19
paragraphs: Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, people of Asian descent have been used as scapegoats in the Western world due to fear of the pandemic and frustrations with changing, inconsistent public health policies globally (Chen, Zhang, and Liu 2020;Li and Nicholson 2021;Reny and Barreto 2022). In mainstream media, Asian imagery has been used to frame the pandemic alongside racialized labeling of COVID-19 as the "Chinese virus" by former US President Donald Trump and other political figures (Darling-Hammond et al. 2020;Kuo et al. 2020). This weaponization of Asian bodies situates them as a persistent threat, and demonstrates the ways in which Asians/Asian Americans occupy a perpetually "marginalized and conditional status" due to longstanding and pervasive Orientalism positioning Western civilization as superior to an exotic East (Li and Nicholson 2021:2). This type of media framing reflects a COVID-19-induced racially hostile media environment (Kuo et al. 2020;Man 2020). Furthering the public imagination of Asians/Asian Americans as forever foreigners within a US racial context through mass media justifies widespread public condemnation of Asian bodies. This form of anti-Asian racism underscores "the historical legacies of whiteness and citizenship" that undergird the US racial imagination (Tessler, Choi, and Kao 2020:638).

Under these constraints, the Stop AAPI Hate Reporting Center launched on March 19, 2020, with the intention to collect data on reports of anti-Asian hate incidences (Takasaki 2020). The Center's latest national report, which elaborates on data collected from March 19, 2020, to March 31, 2022, chronicles 11,467 hate incidents reported against AAPIs (Horse et al. 2022). While not all those who experienced anti-Asian violence were Chinese, due to the West's racialization of Asian bodies which homogenizes cultural and national differences, "the notion of the non-differentiable 'yellow' masses. . .makes possible the transference of Sinophobia to Asian xenophobia" (Siu and Chun 2020:425).

In conjunction with the reporting center's launch and spurred by the rise of media reports of anti-Asian racism, Asians/Asian Americans began using the hashtags #StopAsian-Hate and #StopAAPIHate to chronicle their experiences beyond formal reporting initiatives, with allies drawing attention to the cause in earnest. As part of digital activist strategy, racial justice hashtags like those used during the rise in COVID-related anti-Asian violence have the intention of "demonstrat[ing] injustice, re-fram[ing] discourse, and/or promot[ing] policy change" (Jackson, Bailey, and Welles 2020;Kuo 2018:2;Sharma 2013;Steele 2018). Despite early adoption, usage of these hashtags did not grow en masse until the Atlanta-area TRANSCULTURAL FANDOM IN #STOPASIANHATE 395 spa shootings in March 2021 (Fan, Huizi, and Gilliland 2021;Fischer and Chen 2021). In considering the recurring racist public media narratives about Asian/Asian Americans throughout COVID-19, we ask: what alternative stories about Asians/Asian Americans are being told, where are they being told, and by whom? With increasing numbers of selfreported incidences of racism against Asians/Asian Americans and increased media coverage of anti-Asian violence, researchers must consider how hashtags have the potential to either subvert or codify the racialization of particular groups.

Building off Lopez's (2016) study of Asian American media activists, this paper examines how racial justice activism unfolds online. Existing studies on these particular hashtags, coming largely from the field of computer science, focus on finite time periods during the pandemic, from the immediate weeks following the Atlanta-area shootings (Fan, Huizi, and Gilliland 2021;Lee and Jang 2021;Lyu et al. 2023) to the weeks following US President Biden's signing of the COVID-19 Hate Crimes Act (Cao et al. 2022). Further, researchers have conducted longitudinal examinations of how counter-hate speech, including these hashtags, interacts with anti-Asian hate speech online (He et al. 2020;Lin et al. 2022). Recent research also examines the patterns between the #BlackLivesMatter movement and #StopAsianHate (Tong et al. 2022). A 2022 study focusing on TikTok video content created by Asian/Asian American women examined how #StopAsianHate could function as a discursive space, with messaging around strategies to counter anti-Asian violence and/or forming pan-Asian solidarity amplified through the increased visibility of videos to online publics (Lee and Lee 2023). To explore how conversations shift over time with the ebb and flow of the pandemic, we intentionally use a longer time scale to track the first instances of #StopA-sianHate and #StopAAPIHate at the beginning of the pandemic in March 2020, following the data until the end of May 2022.

In particular, we consider the prevalence of BTS and ARMY's engagement with #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate as indicative of the types of transcultural digital media responses necessary to combat anti-Asian racism. Even with BTS' and ARMY's influence on sustaining these hashtags, the group and its fanbase are largely absent from existing literature examining #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate. The geographic variation in ARMY membership alongside their strong numbers provide a unique opportunity to explore how efforts to counter anti-Asian sentiment can manifest transculturally online in different local contexts. Thus, this work joins emerging scholarship on race, fandom, and activism through cultural production in order to advance our collective understanding of modern digital activism and racial politics.

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: Since its establishment in 2007, Twitter has been the predominant SNS platform used for congregating and disseminating information related to protest across typically unlinked social networks due to its early incorporation of hashtags by its founder (Messina 2007). From #BringBackOurGirls to #OscarsSoWhite, Twitter's platform allows for quick, low-risk involvement in a wide range of social movement dialogue with a potential for mass engagement. Accordingly, even though there are limitations to the data given its concise and informal nature (Hong and Davison 2010), we chose Twitter as our analytical site due to its affordances of with respect to analyzing the social media activity of users globally, since the platform is public and easily scrapeable.

To collect data for analysis, we used the Twint package, an open-source Python library that scrapes public information using Twitter's own search operators in conjunction with the platform's Advanced Search feature (Poldi 2021) to obtain a publicly accessible sample. Twint's increased functionality avoids the use of Twitter's API, which has search and request limitations even for those with developer or academic track access. For each query, we searched the term "#StopAsianHate" or "#StopAAPIHate," filtering out retweets and retaining engagement statistics for each retrieved tweet. While our initial queries focused on one-month ranges at a time, upon noticing some scattered inconsistencies with the time ranges for when data was pulled for each day (the results of which were duplicated when directly inputting queries into Twitter's Advanced Search), we isolated our searches to 1 day at a time for January 1, 2020, through May 31, 2022, then concatenated the days together into files containing the relevant months, years, and an eventual full sample in hopes of collecting as much data as possible. The scope of the detailed queries allows us to examine the long-term resilience and usage patterns of the hashtags. Data collection occurred in early June 2022, and all date ranges were crafted using Eastern Standard Time. Three full datasets were created: one for #StopAsianHate, one for #StopAAPIHate, and one that combined the results of the two datasets, eliminating duplicate rows by filtering on tweet ID. Detailed counts by year are described in Table 1.

To further clean the files, rows that contained duplicate tweet IDs were dropped, ensuring that any cases where tweets were duplicated in the collection process (either by scraping error or by tweets containing both hashtags) were not listed more than once.

Accordingly, even though there are limitations to the data given its concise and informal nature (Hong and Davison 2010), we chose Twitter as our analytical site due to its affordances of with respect to analyzing the social media activity of users globally, since the platform is public and easily scrapeable.
sections.preview_tail: To further clean the files, rows that contained duplicate tweet IDs were dropped, ensuring that any cases where tweets were duplicated in the collection process (either by scraping error or by tweets containing both hashtags) were not listed more than once.

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: Counts for languages are summarized in Table 2. While some previous papers that analyze #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate focus on English tweets only (Cao et al. 2022;Fan, Huizi, and Gilliland 2021;Lyu et al. 2023), we purposefully include tweets in other languages within our full dataset in order to examine the effects of the hashtags on a global scale. This is particularly relevant due to the language barriers that motivate specific forms of anti-Asian discrimination and racism-further, as the COVID-19 pandemic has revealed, the perception of race often lacks nuance when it comes to anti-Asian racism, with many non-Chinese background being broadly classified as Chinese when encountering blame for the pandemic. The language with the most tweets was unsurprisingly English given the US origins of the hashtag, but eight languages had more than 9,000 total tweets. 12.39% of tweets were classified as "qme," meaning that they only contained media (a photo or video) in addition to the hashtag(s), while 11.17% of tweets only contained hashtags and were classified as "qht." An additional 2.61% of tweets were classified as "und," or undefined, meaning that the tweet was linguistically ambiguous or potentially contained more than one language (Trampus 2015).

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: Computational social science provides an opportunity for using computational techniques to advance theories relating to human behavior (Edelmann et al. 2020;Lazer and Radford 2017). Such efforts are uniquely critical in the context of sociology as a discipline, given how the growth of the field will depend on evolving to encompass newer data sources and methodologies (Bail 2014;Edelmann et al. 2020;Evans and Aceves 2016;Golder and Macy 2014;Molina and Garip 2019). Specifically, the rise of big data lends itself to computational tools, such as natural language processing (NLP) and topic modeling, in order to process the massive scale of the data alongside inherent issues like dimensionality. Usergenerated content on social media platforms affords an opportunity to study how people subjectively interact with current events and their communities in a real-time context. To analyze our corpus of data, we use topic modeling, a statistical model, and text mining method based on machine learning techniques that identify similar themes and clusters of words within a corpus in order to analyze unstructured text data. Topic modeling methods assist researchers in identifying topics from corpuses of short-form and long-form text, and advances in methodology over the years have led to the development of supervised, unsupervised, and semi-supervised methods that are used to handle both structured and unstructured data (Albalawi, Yeap, and Benyoucef 2020;Blair, Bi, and Mulvenna 2020;Chen et al. 2019;Egger and Yu 2022;Hong and Davison 2010;Lazer and Radford 2017). However, technological progress has also grappled with challenges inherent to making inferences without guaranteed context (Egger and Yu 2021), particularly with regard to unstructured data and maintaining validity in interpretable results in a social science context. While there exists a long history in the literature of digital activism researchers using topic modeling methods on tweets with a focus on particular hashtags, keywords, or topiccentered accounts (Boon-Itt and Skunkan 2020; Hidayatullah et al. 2018;Lyu, Le Han, and Luli 2021;Surian et al. 2016;Zhao et al. 2011), continued research is needed to better assess the performance of topic modeling techniques on social media data. Accordingly, this work compares the performance of two topic modeling techniques, latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA, a generative statistical model; Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003) and non-negative matrix factorization (NMF, a model based in linear algebra for topic extraction; Lee and Seung 1999). At a high level, LDA fits the parameters of a distributional model of word generation, which assumes that the dataset is generated from underlying topics with different word frequencies. In contrast, NMF is a more direct approach that decomposes the word/ document count matrix into a low-dimensional representation, where the dimensions correspond to the inferred topics. While techniques that use embedding approaches and trained classifiers for topic modeling exist, we focus on LDA and NMF due to the similarities intrinsic to filtering on #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate, hashtags that generally contain related content. Models that are pre-trained on standard English, while more advanced, will likely have a harder time in separating out topics compared to these unsupervised methods, which are forced to try and discriminate due to only being trained on the provided dataset. Tweets from the full dataset were preprocessed using NLP techniques in Python (Bird, Klein, and Loper 2009), where punctuation, emoticons, hyperlinks, and related markup text were removed alongside @s (direct mentions of users) and # symbols (though the hashtag words themselves were kept for the sake of analysis). Tweets with less than four words were eliminated from the analytic sample, which removed a large density of tweets that solely contained one or both hashtags, but no other information. We further restricted our topic modeling analysis to tweets coded as English for interpretability, and a TF-IDF vectorizer was used in the NMF model to weight keywords based on topic relevance. The number of topics (k), assessed for k = 5 to k = 11 topics for both LDA and NMF, was determined after a quantitative assessment of baseline coherence scores for LDA (Rehurek and Sojka 2011) and a qualitative evaluation of the topics generated by NMF. Given the multimodality of LDA, which allows for different results and coherence scores with each run, we conducted multiple trials for each number of topics, and then averaged the results. Through this process, the optimal number of topics was found to be 8 for both methods.

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: We further examined histograms of each month to determine "spikes" in hashtag usage, which we defined as days where there were more than 5,000 tweets using the aggregate of both hashtags. 5,000 tweets were defined as a threshold for analysis based on an examination of day-by-day histograms of the full sample, where 20 of the 29 months had no days with more than 2,000 tweets-accordingly, we assessed 5,000 tweets to be an appropriate lower bound to assess significant increases in discourse. Between March 19, 2020, and May 31, 2022, there were 25 spikes, summarized in Table 3.

The number and dates of these spikes are consistent for #StopAsianHate, but #Sto-pAAPIHate presents a slightly different landscape-the April, May, June, and August 2021 and May 2022 spikes do not exist. As previously mentioned, most uses of #StopAAPIHate that are not promoting the research organization (which appears to be the main focus on AAPI hashtag) are also using #StopAsianHate in the same tweet. Of the 25 spikes, 11 immediately followed the Atlanta-area spa shootings, which generated the largest amount of conversation. Other than that, with the exception of one other event-a match between the Thai and Serbian national volleyball teams where a Serbian player taunted a Thai player with a racist gesture-the remaining 13 spike events were all related to K-pop groups, with 12 events being related to BTS. Four of the BTS-related spikes were tweets of support from fans after BTS' statement reflecting the need to stand against racial discrimination and condemn violence, while the other 8 events were responding to various acts of perceived racism and xenophobia against the group committed by outside actors, mostly radio DJs. The other spike occurred during a virtual concert held in honor of AAPI Heritage month that featured South Korean boy band ATEEZ as a headliner, where the group asked virtual attendees to amplify the hashtag during the concert.

paragraphs: The LDA models are described in Table 5 with the weightage, or importance, of each keyword contributing to the respective topic. Upon examining the output, both authors independently coded each generated topic with overarching themes-two for BTS-related topics, either in defense of or in support of the group, five for topics relating to counter-Asian-hate efforts, and one unspecified general category to reflect a mix of keywords relating to multiple topics-and the results were compared and discussed in order to arrive at a consensus for each topic, with the caveat that the qualitative description of the themes is subjective and informed by the events described in Table 3. Interactive LDA visualizations made with LDAvis (Chuang, Manning, and Heer 2012;Sievert and Shirley 2014) that allow for exploration of the top 30 most salient keywords per topic alongside an adjustable relevance metric are available for download at tinyurl.com/SAHLDA2, and static images of the visualizations are available in Appendix S1.

being able to see the density of a particular term within all the generated topics). Even so, the graphics illuminate that even for k = 8, the generated topics can be quite noisy, with a significant amount of keyword overlap in where words are propagating, particularly if they were more general terms like "racism" that were relevant to many threads of conversation. This emphasizes a weakness of LDA in that its basis on word frequency may lead to insights that are not useful without sufficient background information about the corpus.

The NMF model similarly suffered from some noise in keywords, particularly as the number of topics were increased-however, for k = 8, the individually generated topics were more distinct and interpretable, particularly in with our descriptive results, which gave us preliminary insight into which topics might be relevant within the data. As we iterated increasing numbers of topics, we found that it was easier to disaggregate multiple 412 AMY ZHANG, AND CHRISTINA ONG 1475682x, 2024, 2, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/soin.12590 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [02/02/2026]. perspectives on the same discourse through NMF; for example, we were able to classify different types of discursive themes related to the Stop Asian Hate movement. One salient difference between the two models was the presence of more specific keywords related to the Atlanta-area shootings within the LDA models compared to the NMF ones, which lacked terms we expected (i.e., "Atlanta" or "victims"), even though a topic did reference how a police captain summarized the perpetrator's actions as the result of a "very bad day." This could be the result of people talking about the events in different ways, particularly since tweets containing media such as links, images, and videos were excluded from this analysis. That said, the NMF model pulled out more specific phrases, and did not shy away from foreign hashtags (likely categorized as English due to the lack of language-specific identifiers, or the presence of largely English words), leading to differences within the models that emphasized the strengths of NMF over LDA. Specifically, the NMF models revealed more specific nuances within unspecified categories that contained multiple topics-for example, topics within Corpus 2 and Corpus 3 mentioned "tacha" and "sonny," referring to Nigerian influencer Natacha "Tacha" Akide and acclaimed Tottenham Hotspur soccer player Son Heung-min, respectively. Their inclusions further the influence of #StopA-sianHate within a global context with nods to different forms of fandom, whether in the form of advocacy and support through an influencer's actions or in visibility for condemning racist vitriol directed at an athlete in reaction to his talents. Even though our results affirm previous literature (Egger and Yu 2022) and indicate support for NMF over LDA in analyzing Twitter data, we were only able to interpret some topics due to prior knowledge of current events relating to anti-Asian sentiment and K-pop, and additional research should be conducted using a wider range of topics in order to ascertain differences between the methods.

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: Our goal in conducting this analysis was to examine the role of viral hashtags in the conversation around stances on anti-Asian racism. Through collecting Twitter data using the Twint Python package, we were able to visualize the varying amounts of discourse that happened over the course of the COVID-19 pandemic alongside what types of events triggered spikes in conversation. Overall, given the patterns of tweet density between March 2020 and May 2022, intentional engagement with a viral hashtag in response to conflicts or tragedy did not appear to contribute to long-lasting discourse, with almost all spikes dipping back down to more stagnant levels within a day or two. We anticipated that certain dates regarding notable legislation (like May 20, 2021, when President Biden signed the COVID-19 Hate Crimes Act) or dates of tragedies that received national media attention (such as January 15, 2022, the murder of Michelle Alyssa Go in a New York City subway) would result in spikes or at least increased engagement with these hashtags; however, none of the newsworthy events that we examined outside of the Atlanta-area spa shootings yielded significant spikes within the hashtags.

The month with the most engagement for either hashtag, March 2021, was characterized by three main events, with the most sustained period of spikes being the days after the Atlanta-area spa shootings, and the other two relating to BTS. Compared to hashtag activism used with the #BlackLivesMatter movement that escalated after the murder of Michael Brown in 2014, there are similarities with regard to the narrative power of people reacting with anger, sharing their experiences and stories, and using the hashtag in order to highlight the need for action and change (Freelon, McIlwain, and Clark 2016;Yang 2016). However, unlike the #BlackLivesMatter movement, which coalesced around a formalized organization with a clear agenda and policy demands, #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate lack discernable goals. Due to Asian American racial justice hashtag usage being led by individual actors rather than organizations, there has been no clear consensus regarding its usage or intended aims, even as people seek community through collective participation and center messages around similar themes of visibility, awareness, representation, and justice (Lopez 2016). For some, using these hashtags is an entrypoint into further political education, while for others, engagement may stop once these hashtags lose their virality.

As with many hashtags used for activist purposes, initial attention was minimal, temporal, and episodic, with a lag between the inception of the hashtag and any sort of widespread use of it. However, after March 2021, neither #StopAsianHate nor #StopAAPIHate captured any kind of momentum remotely similar to the initial significant spike in attention on March 17, 2021, as further emphasized by highly publicized acts of anti-Asian violence not being well-captured in our defined spikes. As the elaborated spike events demonstrate, the hashtags' momentum remains greatest around highly publicized acts (whether this publicity is media-driven or fan-driven) of violence or discrimination, two concepts that are often intertwined.

Focusing on the NMF results given the additional nuance, Table 5 reveals that BTSspecific topics dominated almost every date range, with six of eight coded topics in Corpuses 1 and 5 and five of eight topics in Corpuses 3 and 4 pertaining to BTS. Corpus 2, which was focused on the dates following the Atlanta-area shootings, was the only date range where five of the eight topics related to countering anti-Asian sentiment. Overall, of 24 BTS-related topics, 20 were coded as being defensive of the group, reflecting the prevailing use of the hashtags in condemning racism against BTS. Considering 60% of the identified topics pertained to BTS, ARMY's persistent usage of the hashtags motivated the staying power of each topic, revealing how fan engagement can correlate with a trend's longevity.

March 2021 further reveals insights regarding the contributions of fandom through the two BTS-related spikes at the beginning and end of the month. As described in Table 3 and supported through the topic modeling results for Corpus 1 in Tables 4 and 5, the March 1 spike largely focuses on backlash against a German radio disc jockey who took issue with BTS covering a Coldplay song. When the day's tweets are inspected, it is clear that other discourse exists within the tweets, with people trying to grapple with increases in violence and discrimination against Asians/Asian Americans alongside organizations and companies releasing statements of support for communities. This is exemplified in Figure 3b, the most retweeted tweet from March 1, which did not come from a BTS fan account (though it was an account supporting a different K-pop group, Seventeen), alongside Figure 3c, the secondmost retweeted tweet from that day, which came from a BTS fan account sharing #StopA-sianHate resources. However, these general counter-hate tweets were insignificant in number against the flood of tweets defending BTS, most of which used a widely spread template message that included other event-specific hashtags such as "#NoRacismInMedia." Given this event occurred both in Germany and before the Atlanta-area shootings, this spike demonstrates that non-US fans understood the meaning and potential for virality inherent to the hashtag and how to use it in relation to BTS (even if other uses of it were US-focused) before BTS themselves used the hashtag. While the use of templated messages including #NoRacismInMedia foreclose a more nuanced discussion of anti-Asian racism, ARMY is TRANSCULTURAL FANDOM IN #STOPASIANHATE 415 witnessing and responding in real time to the ways that BTS become sites of racialization in different local contexts, albeit always influenced by the West's orientalist fantasies.

The March 29 spike, dense enough to create a spike more than 175% that of the next day's in just over 2 hours, showed the immediacy with which ARMY would respond to a BTS tweet (which many of them likely had notifications set up for), particularly when it was one relating to a topic this serious. March 29's tweets reveal that both ARMY and people who did not identify specifically as ARMY respected BTS for using their significant platform to make a long, careful statement about countering anti-Asian sentiment, given the ease with which they could have chosen to say nothing (Lee 2022). BTS' transcultural popularity, novel in its global penetration for K-pop, situates them in a unique place of vulnerability with regard to public exposure-however, the strength of their fanbase is reflected through a willingness to employ digital strategies to support and advocate for the group in the face of racism, as demonstrated through the spikes present in the rest of the dataset.

While engagement in defending favorite groups from hate incidents might be occurring outside of hashtags, the amplification of #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate by ARMY to further awareness of perceived discrimination was one of the main drivers of the persistence of these hashtags over time. As described in Table 3, ARMY often used template messages disseminated by larger fan accounts to repeatedly emphasize the same message, which often contained hashtags in the relevant language in addition to #StopAsianHate-this group amplification is evidenced within our topic models through typos in some keywords (i.e., "racism"). This process may lead some to inquire if there is room for genuine engagement with anti-racism if ARMY amplify tweets en masse in response to any perceived slight against BTS. In many ways, the usage of #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate by ARMY echoes the limitations of other racial justice hashtags, as there is often little room for nuance due to the way that it "alters our consciousness by being shorthand for complicated social, political, and economic issues" (Crandall and Cunningham 2016:25). The abbreviated nature of racial justice hashtags, particularly in the context of Twitter's character limit, often obscures "important facets of the cause such as historical background of socio-political context" (Dadas 2017:18). Still, understanding that ARMY has had sustained, long-term engagement in online spaces even prior to COVID-19 reveals more possibilities than closures.

As anti-Asian sentiments spread, so too do the tactics to counter them. As such, it is important to trace the transcultural effects of logics that may otherwise be perceived as USor Western-centric. Despite being originally created and promoted by Asian Americans, #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate only persisted due to the efforts of people highlighting anti-Asian sentiment in places that were not the US, demonstrating that this form of racism is not unique to the West; nor do they only impact Asian bodies in Western worlds. Regardless of one's opinion on the importance of K-pop music, the potential power of fandom in motivating social media engagement must be considered, particularly in light of how several of the hashtag spike events were driven by non-US ARMY reacting to instances of anti-Asian racism directed at BTS in countries with a low concentration of citizens of Asian descent such as Germany, the Dominican Republic, Chile, and Colombia. Dismissing ARMY and their efforts based on preconceived notions of what and who K-pop fans are is rooted in misogyny and ageism (Cruz 2019;Lancaster 2015). Instead, we argue that fandoms like ARMY are capable and well-informed with the potential for more immersive engagement beyond hashtag amplification. If fans of a megagroup like BTS can significantly boost the popularity of racial justice hashtags to the point where the terms trend worldwide and potentially yield increased local influence, there is opportunity in seeing fandom as a powerful driver for change, as has been previously documented in the case of COVID-19 public health messaging (Chang, Pham, and Ferrara 2021). Social justice-minded individuals should consider how best to harness the energy of potential allies without dismissing individuals for where their initial motivation for contributing to activism originates.

BTS' transcultural popularity, novel in its global penetration for K-pop, situates them in a unique place of vulnerability with regard to public exposure-however, the strength of their fanbase is reflected through a willingness to employ digital strategies to support and advocate for the group in the face of racism, as demonstrated through the spikes present in the rest of the dataset.

While engagement in defending favorite groups from hate incidents might be occurring outside of hashtags, the amplification of #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate by ARMY to further awareness of perceived discrimination was one of the main drivers of the persistence of these hashtags over time. As described in Table 3, ARMY often used template messages disseminated by larger fan accounts to repeatedly emphasize the same message, which often contained hashtags in the relevant language in addition to #StopAsianHate-this group amplification is evidenced within our topic models through typos in some keywords (i.e., "racism"). This process may lead some to inquire if there is room for genuine engagement with anti-racism if ARMY amplify tweets en masse in response to any perceived slight against BTS. In many ways, the usage of #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate by ARMY echoes the limitations of other racial justice hashtags, as there is often little room for nuance due to the way that it "alters our consciousness by being shorthand for complicated social, political, and economic issues" (Crandall and Cunningham 2016:25). The abbreviated nature of racial justice hashtags, particularly in the context of Twitter's character limit, often obscures "important facets of the cause such as historical background of socio-political context" (Dadas 2017:18). Still, understanding that ARMY has had sustained, long-term engagement in online spaces even prior to COVID-19 reveals more possibilities than closures.

As anti-Asian sentiments spread, so too do the tactics to counter them. As such, it is important to trace the transcultural effects of logics that may otherwise be perceived as USor Western-centric. Despite being originally created and promoted by Asian Americans, #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate only persisted due to the efforts of people highlighting anti-Asian sentiment in places that were not the US, demonstrating that this form of racism is not unique to the West; nor do they only impact Asian bodies in Western worlds. Regardless of one's opinion on the importance of K-pop music, the potential power of fandom in motivating social media engagement must be considered, particularly in light of how several of the hashtag spike events were driven by non-US ARMY reacting to instances of anti-Asian racism directed at BTS in countries with a low concentration of citizens of Asian descent such as Germany, the Dominican Republic, Chile, and Colombia. Dismissing ARMY and their efforts based on preconceived notions of what and who K-pop fans are is rooted in misogyny and ageism (Cruz 2019;Lancaster 2015). Instead, we argue that fandoms like ARMY are capable and well-informed with the potential for more immersive engagement beyond hashtag amplification. If fans of a megagroup like BTS can significantly boost the popularity of racial justice hashtags to the point where the terms trend worldwide and potentially yield increased local influence, there is opportunity in seeing fandom as a powerful driver for change, as has been previously documented in the case of COVID-19 public health messaging (Chang, Pham, and Ferrara 2021). Social justice-minded individuals should consider how best to harness the energy of potential allies without dismissing individuals for where their initial motivation for contributing to activism originates.

Our goal in conducting this analysis was to examine the role of viral hashtags in the conversation around stances on anti-Asian racism. Through collecting Twitter data using the Twint Python package, we were able to visualize the varying amounts of discourse that happened over the course of the COVID-19 pandemic alongside what types of events triggered spikes in conversation. Overall, given the patterns of tweet density between March 2020 and May 2022, intentional engagement with a viral hashtag in response to conflicts or tragedy did not appear to contribute to long-lasting discourse, with almost all spikes dipping back down to more stagnant levels within a day or two. We anticipated that certain dates regarding notable legislation (like May 20, 2021, when President Biden signed the COVID-19 Hate Crimes Act) or dates of tragedies that received national media attention (such as January 15, 2022, the murder of Michelle Alyssa Go in a New York City subway) would result in spikes or at least increased engagement with these hashtags; however, none of the newsworthy events that we examined outside of the Atlanta-area spa shootings yielded significant spikes within the hashtags.

The month with the most engagement for either hashtag, March 2021, was characterized by three main events, with the most sustained period of spikes being the days after the Atlanta-area spa shootings, and the other two relating to BTS. Compared to hashtag activism used with the #BlackLivesMatter movement that escalated after the murder of Michael Brown in 2014, there are similarities with regard to the narrative power of people reacting with anger, sharing their experiences and stories, and using the hashtag in order to highlight the need for action and change (Freelon, McIlwain, and Clark 2016;Yang 2016). However, unlike the #BlackLivesMatter movement, which coalesced around a formalized organization with a clear agenda and policy demands, #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate lack discernable goals. Due to Asian American racial justice hashtag usage being led by individual actors rather than organizations, there has been no clear consensus regarding its usage or intended aims, even as people seek community through collective participation and center messages around similar themes of visibility, awareness, representation, and justice (Lopez 2016). For some, using these hashtags is an entrypoint into further political education, while for others, engagement may stop once these hashtags lose their virality.

As with many hashtags used for activist purposes, initial attention was minimal, temporal, and episodic, with a lag between the inception of the hashtag and any sort of widespread use of it. However, after March 2021, neither #StopAsianHate nor #StopAAPIHate captured any kind of momentum remotely similar to the initial significant spike in attention on March 17, 2021, as further emphasized by highly publicized acts of anti-Asian violence not being well-captured in our defined spikes. As the elaborated spike events demonstrate, the hashtags' momentum remains greatest around highly publicized acts (whether this publicity is media-driven or fan-driven) of violence or discrimination, two concepts that are often intertwined.

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: This work is not without limitations. By nature of scraping publicly available data, the process is vulnerable to inconsistencies in query returns on Twitter's end-for example, in the exploratory phase of our data collection, we discovered irregularities with data scraping that occurred when trying to obtain data from certain months in 2021, where tweets would only appear from half of the day. To maximize the amount of data obtained, instead of scraping data month by month, we scraped by day and then aggregated the data togetherhowever, it is possible that there was still some loss, particularly since the inconsistencies were replicated when doing manual searches. An informal conversation with a Twitter software engineer in August 2021 revealed that they also were unsure why the error was happening. Such concerns are often downsides of conducting research using publicly available data, and future research should attempt a more thorough cleaning process to ascertain as much data as possible.

Further, since we are only accessing public data, we cannot account for tweets from accounts that are private (or were made private/otherwise suspended or deleted between when the tweet was made and when the data was collected). Additionally, during our data scraping process, we only collected tweets that included #StopAsianHate or #StopAAPIHate, but there is certainly extensive discourse happening that does not use these hashtags. While we acknowledge these as limitations, the use of a hashtag in itself implies a specific desire to participate in dialogue-either by contributing to a conversation by amplifying the use of the same hashtag, or to counter dialogue happening in a densely populated hashtag. Trying to keep track of conversations around a particular topic that does not include a focal point or keywords, such as a hashtag, becomes quite difficult. While other papers that have worked on similar themes attempted to counter this by expanding the range of their search to include keywords like "asian" or "aapi", we believe that including such terms is too broad of a scope when assessing the landscape of conversation pertaining to this topic (Lyu et al. 2023). Posts invoking hashtags can further be volatile in nature due to the structure of sites like Twitter, where all communication is broadcast to an audience and polyvocality may drown out attempts at unified messaging, which is particularly unfortunate in topics that require nuance or complexity (Brock 2012;Lopez 2016).

One additional limitation to this work is the lack of disaggregation by race or gender, especially considering that a majority of ARMY are women (Grover et al. 2022). While we wish to examine these topics under a lens that incorporates these demographic characteristics, such work is difficult without making generalized inferences, and there is not a uniformly accepted method for ascertaining the race or ethnicity of social media users (Golder et al. 2022;Jung et al. 2018). For example, many papers in computer science that examine Internet data use facial recognition or artificial intelligence (AI) software in order to derive the gender and race of respondents from Twitter profiles. Such uses of this software commonly rely on simplistic definitions and stereotypes, such as skin tone generalization or an overreliance on gender stereotypes that are not nuanced in the inclusion of identities deviant TRANSCULTURAL FANDOM IN #STOPASIANHATE 417 from binary definitions of masculinity and femininity (Crawford and Paglen 2021;Leslie 2020). In the case of fan accounts, many users utilize photos of their idols or logos instead of their own photos on their profile. Even methods that use text as a basis, such as classifying race based on defined names, run into issues with the anonymity that Twitter can provide-unless one restricts the sample to primarily verified accounts, it is difficult to verify the sample. Additional work is needed in this vein to account for these potential sources of variation in an ethical way, balancing whether methods that make a "best approximation" are worth it in the context of not contributing to continuing hegemony.

sections.paper_id: Zhang and Ong - 2024 - “We Are Bulletproof” The Transcultural Power of Fandom in #
paragraphs: Overall, we find that our dataset yields high potential for continued research on how people engage with topics concerning anti-Asian rhetoric. While large spikes in conversation occurred after the Atlanta-area spa shootings, the hashtags ultimately did not have the same conversational staying power as racial justice hashtags like #BlackLivesMatter. Regardless, based on the 12 spikes in #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate usage from BTS-related events, it is clear that fandom serves as a point of entry for participation in collective digital activism focused on countering anti-Asian racism. We observe that ARMY is motivated to counter negative characterizations of BTS precisely because of their dedication to the group. People engaged in large numbers to virtually protest instances of racism against BTS-so much so that these hashtags had longevity, but mainly as a reflection of the fandom's dedication. While critical, much of this engagement is readily dismissed or overlooked altogether because of the ways that BTS themselves are racialized as Asians, alongside negative perceptions of why their fans are drawn to them.

Though ARMY engages en masse with #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate, the use of certain fan strategies such as mass-templated messages can limit discussion of nuanced perspectives on anti-Asian racism that fans may hold, particularly if fans do so indiscriminately on the suggestion of popular accounts or just through seeing others doing so. Accordingly, different methods are necessary to properly assess if ARMY's online engagement with topics of racial solidarity pertaining to BTS translates to offline awareness and direct action. Further, even though we retained all amplified duplicate tweets to get a sense of scale, analytic datasets could be created that remove duplicate text in order to better visualize unique discourse. Beyond assessing improved methods for filtering tweets by various demographic traits, additional research could be conducted on verified versus non-verified users to see what forms of conversation are sustained on Twitter based on an individual's positionality.

As supported by our findings, engagement with ideas countering anti-Asian racism can be insular and restricted to specific networks. More research should be conducted to examine how conversation can be sustained without the catalyst of an adverse event or a national organization solely devoted to activist solutions. Social media engagement in K-pop fandom communities also provides an extensive playground for studies on online discourse that have yet to be thoroughly explored-academics should consider the politics of fanwars and multiplicative toxicity as a case study in online engagement for future research, given how differing perceptions of intent can impact the effects of digital activism.

Overall, we sought to longitudinally examine the uses of #StopAsianHate and #Sto-pAAPIHate, identifying how the hashtags were used over time and by whom. Our results revealed that a significant contributor to the longevity of #StopAsianHate and StopAAPIHate is the BTS ARMY, who strategically amplify shared sentiment through mass engagement with the viral hashtags alongside unified stances and concurring hashtag messaging. Even though these efforts can bring awareness to incidents of xenophobia and discrimination, such efforts can only go so far in motivating continued engagement if those efforts are disregarded outside of insular fandom communities. The tradition of writing off "boy bands" and their fans, as couched in patriarchal ideals of what constitutes reasonable obsession, is further complicated by BTS' Asianness in conjunction with their boundary-pushing achievements, which belie Western conceptions of cultural advancement. Even so, the documented economic and social impact of BTS (ex. regular fan-led global donation campaigns in their honor and the members speaking at the UN General Assembly and White House about social issues that matter to them, including countering anti-Asian sentiment) should indicate that the group and their fandom contribute something innovative to the realm of digital activism-even if BTS themselves have never claimed to be activists. While some may disqualify BTS as a source of activist motivation without knowing anything about them, their "unlikely trajectory is a natural result of their authenticity, artistry, and social conscientiousness" (Cruz 2023). Just as with BTS' unlikely trajectory, the online advocacy of ARMY across the world reveals an unexpected, albeit powerful, potential mechanism for transculturally mediated anti-racist digital activism.

Overall, we find that our dataset yields high potential for continued research on how people engage with topics concerning anti-Asian rhetoric. While large spikes in conversation occurred after the Atlanta-area spa shootings, the hashtags ultimately did not have the same conversational staying power as racial justice hashtags like #BlackLivesMatter. Regardless, based on the 12 spikes in #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate usage from BTS-related events, it is clear that fandom serves as a point of entry for participation in collective digital activism focused on countering anti-Asian racism. We observe that ARMY is motivated to counter negative characterizations of BTS precisely because of their dedication to the group. People engaged in large numbers to virtually protest instances of racism against BTS-so much so that these hashtags had longevity, but mainly as a reflection of the fandom's dedication. While critical, much of this engagement is readily dismissed or overlooked altogether because of the ways that BTS themselves are racialized as Asians, alongside negative perceptions of why their fans are drawn to them.

Though ARMY engages en masse with #StopAsianHate and #StopAAPIHate, the use of certain fan strategies such as mass-templated messages can limit discussion of nuanced perspectives on anti-Asian racism that fans may hold, particularly if fans do so indiscriminately on the suggestion of popular accounts or just through seeing others doing so. Accordingly, different methods are necessary to properly assess if ARMY's online engagement with topics of racial solidarity pertaining to BTS translates to offline awareness and direct action. Further, even though we retained all amplified duplicate tweets to get a sense of scale, analytic datasets could be created that remove duplicate text in order to better visualize unique discourse. Beyond assessing improved methods for filtering tweets by various demographic traits, additional research could be conducted on verified versus non-verified users to see what forms of conversation are sustained on Twitter based on an individual's positionality.

As supported by our findings, engagement with ideas countering anti-Asian racism can be insular and restricted to specific networks. More research should be conducted to examine how conversation can be sustained without the catalyst of an adverse event or a national organization solely devoted to activist solutions. Social media engagement in K-pop fandom communities also provides an extensive playground for studies on online discourse that have yet to be thoroughly explored-academics should consider the politics of fanwars and multiplicative toxicity as a case study in online engagement for future research, given how differing perceptions of intent can impact the effects of digital activism.

Overall, we sought to longitudinally examine the uses of #StopAsianHate and #Sto-pAAPIHate, identifying how the hashtags were used over time and by whom. Our results revealed that a significant contributor to the longevity of #StopAsianHate and StopAAPIHate is the BTS ARMY, who strategically amplify shared sentiment through mass engagement with the viral hashtags alongside unified stances and concurring hashtag messaging. Even though these efforts can bring awareness to incidents of xenophobia and discrimination, such efforts can only go so far in motivating continued engagement if those efforts are disregarded outside of insular fandom communities. The tradition of writing off "boy bands" and their fans, as couched in patriarchal ideals of what constitutes reasonable obsession, is further complicated by BTS' Asianness in conjunction with their boundary-pushing achievements, which belie Western conceptions of cultural advancement. Even so, the documented economic and social impact of BTS (ex. regular fan-led global donation campaigns in their honor and the members speaking at the UN General Assembly and White House about social issues that matter to them, including countering anti-Asian sentiment) should indicate that the group and their fandom contribute something innovative to the realm of digital activism-even if BTS themselves have never claimed to be activists. While some may disqualify BTS as a source of activist motivation without knowing anything about them, their "unlikely trajectory is a natural result of their authenticity, artistry, and social conscientiousness" (Cruz 2023). Just as with BTS' unlikely trajectory, the online advocacy of ARMY across the world reveals an unexpected, albeit powerful, potential mechanism for transculturally mediated anti-racist digital activism.
